title: 2026 Grammy Record of the Year Nominees
text: The eight nominees for Record of the Year at the 2026 Grammy Awards showcase diverse musical styles and significant commercial success from 2025. Bad Bunny — "DtMF": The title track from Bad Bunny's album "Debí Tirar Más Fotos" blends Puerto Rico's plena music with video-game beats. The song resonated deeply with audiences, becoming one of his biggest hits and reaching No. 1 on multiple charts. Sabrina Carpenter — "Manchild": Following her successful 2024, Carpenter released this country-inspired synth-pop track as the lead single from her seventh album. The witty song marked her first No. 1 debut on the Billboard Hot 100. Doechii — "Anxiety": Originally uploaded to YouTube in 2019, this track went viral through TikTok in 2025. Built on a sample from Gotye's "Somebody That I Used to Know," it became Doechii's first top 10 hit on the Billboard Hot 100. Billie Eilish — "WILDFLOWER": The fourth single from Eilish's 2024 album, this folk-pop ballad became her longest-charting song, spending 72 weeks on the Billboard Hot 100. Co-written with brother FINNEAS, it explores themes of unrequited love and emotional vulnerability. Lady Gaga — "Abracadabra": This dark club anthem from her album "MAYHEM" marked Gaga's triumphant 2025 return. The hypnotic track debuted during the 2025 Grammy broadcast and climbed to No. 5 on the Billboard Global 200. Kendrick Lamar with SZA — "Luther": Sampling Luther Vandross and Cheryl Lynn's 1982 song, this collaboration became a commercial force, spending 13 consecutive weeks at No. 1 on the Billboard Hot 100—the second-longest hip-hop chart run ever. Chappell Roan — "The Subway": This alt-pop track about encountering an ex-lover debuted at No. 3 on the Billboard Hot 100, becoming Roan's highest-charting single. The artist initially hesitated releasing a studio version, believing it worked better live. ROSÉ & Bruno Mars — "APT.": Inspired by a Korean drinking game, this infectious pop-punk anthem topped charts in over 50 countries. ROSÉ became the highest-charting female K-pop soloist in the U.S. and the first K-pop artist nominated for Record of the Year.
